bibliographic_data.title: Collaborative Attention Mechanism for Multi-View Action Recognition
bibliographic_data.abstract: Multi-view action recognition (MVAR) leverages complementary temporal information from different views to improve the learning performance. Obtaining informative view-specific representation plays an essential role in MVAR. Attention has been widely adopted as an effective strategy for discovering discriminative cues underlying temporal data. However, most existing MVAR methods only utilize attention to extract representation for each view individually, ignoring the potential to dig latent patterns based on mutual-support information in attention space. To this end, we propose a collaborative attention mechanism (CAM) for solving the MVAR problem in this paper. The proposed CAM detects the attention differences among multi-view and adaptively integrates frame-level information to benefit each other.

core_content.novelty_claim: We propose a collaborative attention mechanism (CAM) framework to improve the MVAR performance, which utilizes attention information across different views to mutually enhance multi-view learning.
core_content.key_methodology: The proposed CAM detects the attention differences among multiple views and adaptively integrates frame-level information to benefit each other. Specifically, we extend the long short-term memory (LSTM) to a Mutual-Aid RNN (MAR) to achieve the multi-view collaboration process.
outcomes: Achieves better results for each view
core_content.full_text_markdown: Multi-view action recognition (MVAR) has become an important task due to its ability to leverage complementary temporal information from different views. However, most existing methods only utilize attention to extract representation for each view individually, ignoring the potential to discover latent patterns based on mutual-support information in attention space. To address this issue, we propose a collaborative attention mechanism (CAM) that detects the attention differences among multiple views and adaptively integrates frame-level information to benefit each other. Specifically, we extend the long short-term memory (LSTM) to a Mutual-Aid RNN (MAR) to achieve multi-view collaboration. The proposed CAM takes advantage of view-specific attention patterns to guide another view and discover potential information which is hard to be explored by itself. This approach paves a novel way to leverage attention information and enhances the multi-view representation learning. Extensive experiments on four action datasets illustrate that the proposed CAM achieves better results for each view and also boosts multi-view performance.
